summary: Stellar Dark Star → Morning Dew and tight second‑set gems
blurb: The May 23 1972 London show delivers a soaring Dark Star that morphs into an emotional Morning Dew, followed by solid renditions of He's Gone, Sugar Magnolia and Comes A Time. Jerry’s guitar shines, Phil lays down a warm, menacing bass foundation, and the band stays locked in throughout both sets.
review: On May 23, 1972 at London’s Strand Lyceum the Dead rode high on one of the spring‑’72 tour’s most memorable second‑sets. The centerpiece is an epic Dark Star that bursts into a heart‑wrenching Morning Dew, a sequence repeatedly called “definitive” and “out‑of‑this‑world” by listeners. From there the band rolls through a tight He’s Gone, a jubilant Sugar Magnolia and a beautifully sung Comes A Time before closing the night with a rollicking double Not Fade Away/Hey Bo Diddley medley and an encore of Uncle John’s Band. Across all six soundboard sources the performance is praised for its crystal‑clear mix, balanced instrument separation and Jerry Garcia’s soaring lead tones that sit perfectly atop Phil Lesh’s warm, menacing bass lines. Keith Godchaux’s organ on The Stranger (Two Souls in Communion) and his piano work on Chinatown Shuffle add extra color, while Bill Kreutzmann’s “devil‑sliding” toms drive the Dark Star jam into a Fantasia‑like crescendo. Even the recordings with minor hiss or speed‑correction quirks capture the show’s energy, making this night a must‑listen for any Deadhead.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six recordings are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly praising the Dark Star → Morning Dew sequence, Two Souls in Communion, and tight band playing. No consistent negative comments appear; only one mixed note about a single Dark Star take. The volume of strong praise across multiple sources justifies a high positive sentiment adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent stereo soundboard capture with crystal‑clear clarity; praised as the definitive Dark Star → Morning Dew recording and noted for its seamless second‑set flow.

key_highlights: Definitive Dark Star → Morning Dew sequence that’s repeatedly called “out of this world.”
Two Souls in Communion (The Stranger) showcases Pigpen’s organ/piano brilliance.
Tight, energetic second set featuring He’s Gone, Sugar Magnolia and Comes A Time.
High‑quality soundboard mix across all sources; even the fair‑quality versions retain the show’s energy.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar is described as soaring, out‑of‑this‑world and “one of rock’s greatest lead‑guitarists,” especially during the Dark Star jam and its transition into Morning Dew.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh delivers a warm, menacing bass foundation that underpins the improvisations; his tone on Dark Star is highlighted as exceptional.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s rhythm work shines on early Dark Star passages and his vocal on Jack Straw is called “brilliant,” adding solid support throughout the set.
band_performance.Keith: Godchaux’s organ/piano adds color to Two Souls in Communion and Chinatown Shuffle, earning specific praise for texture and drive.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s toms are described as “devil‑sliding” and his drumming during Dark Star is wild, expansive, and a key part of the jam’s power.
